items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: How is an igneous rock formed?
items.random_wrong: By heating and pressing other rocks
items.why: The answer examiners want is: From cooled magma or lava. “From sediment layers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which type of rock is marble?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Metamorphic. “Igneous” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main types of rock are there in the rock cycle?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
